progGevorderd3: For the assignment of the Advanced Programming 3 course in my studies the application had to meet many requirements, which we will now discuss.
frontendOntwikkeling: Currently working on the frontend of the application. (A video of the progress coming soon!)
h2HistoriekDevops: Complete version control history via DevOps
cosmosDb: We started developing InterieurSim with a cloud-based Product API that stores its data in an Azure Cosmos database. We chose Cosmos because it is a NoSQL database that allows the system to quickly look up product data even when there are many product instances. The products have no mutual relationships. In our application, the user can search a catalog of thousands of products to arrange their interior, with products assigned positions in the room. The ratio between products and positions is extremely large, so a relational database is not suitable.
postMethod: Below is the POST method that sends product data from the client to the server.
productService: This data is then forwarded and processed by the ProductService.
productRepo: The data is stored in the Cosmos DB via the repository.
positionApi: The Position API is set up in a similar way.
dtos: To clearly define which information the client provides to create a Product and which information the server ultimately stores, we write a contract to list these requirements.
responseContract: (We also add a contract to specify which data the server sends back to the client.)
productBlobImg: The Cosmos product data has a corresponding product image, which is stored in Azure Blob Storage.
authPostmanDuende: We authorize and authenticate Postman to use the Product API and Position API, so our products cannot be used in any third-party app. We use Duende Identity Server, a .NET framework for implementing the OpenID and OAuth protocols. The framework standardizes and simplifies the implementation of these protocols.
identityServerBackendServer: The Identity Server is a backend server that ensures proper authorization and authentication of app users.
buildServicesAppPipeline: With its configuration in build services and the app pipeline, which are executed when Identity Server starts. Here we configure, among other things, simple logging and the ability to verify JWT tokens.
jwtTokens: JWT tokens are keys that the Identity Server sends to the client for API authorization.
appClients: We also configure the app clients. These app clients are programs for which we set up their authorization to the various APIs. For now, we only give Postman full access to the APIs if the correct JWT token is authorized.
addUniqueUsers: Next, we add users to access the APIs via a React application. The user clicks login in the React app, is redirected to the Webappauth app and then to the Identity Server. Here, the user logs in and is then sent back to the React app as a logged-in user.
identityResources: We then create Identity resources that provide profiles with a unique ID to enable the creation of unique users.
configIdentityServIdentityResources: Identity resources configuration in Config.cs from IdentityServer
appClientWebappAuth: Next, we add an app client for the WebappAuth app.
entityFramework: We link the SQL user database with Identity Server for authentication via Entity Framework. We use a simple UserDbContext that inherits the necessary methods from IdentityDbContext (NuGet package Microsoft.AspNetCore.Identity.EntityFrameworkCore).
entityFrameworkConfigureServices: Code in the configureServices method in HostingExtensions.cs of IdentityServer
initializeDatabase: New InitializeDatabase method in HostingExtensions.cs from IdentityServer, called in the app pipeline configuration.
cookies: Cookies were also set to keep the user logged in. This falls outside the scope of what I explain here.
limitRequests: We want to add throttling/rate limiting so too many users do not overload the system. For this example, we set a limit of 100 requests every 10 seconds.
whatsnextAddFrontend: Currently, I am working on adding the frontend application.
